perturbation.original_text: Each party shall take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information of the other party. Without limiting the foregoing, each party shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own most highly confidential information, and shall promptly notify the disclosing party of any misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information of which it becomes aware.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall endeavor to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information of the other party. Without limiting the foregoing, each party shall attempt to take measures to protect its own most highly confidential information, and shall notify the disclosing party of any confirmed misuse or misappropriation of Confidential Information of which it becomes aware.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'shall take reasonable measures,' creating a definite obligation. The modified text uses 'shall endeavor to protect,' weakening the obligation and making it ambiguous. 'Attempt to take measures' further dilutes the responsibility. The term 'confirmed misuse' introduces ambiguity as it is not stated who is responsible for confirming misuse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 812.081 - Theft of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The Florida statute on theft of trade secrets implies a duty of care to protect confidential information. By changing 'shall take reasonable measures' to 'shall endeavor to protect' and 'attempt to take measures', the contract provides a loophole where a party might not be held fully accountable for failing to adequately protect trade secrets. This weakened obligation could hinder the enforcement of the statute in a civil or criminal case involving misappropriation of confidential information, since proving a willful violation becomes difficult.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Trade secrets; theft, embezzlement; unlawful copying; definitions; penalty. — (1) As used in this section: (a) “Article” means any object, device, machine, material, substance, or composition of matter, or any mixture or copy thereof, whether in whole or in part, including any complete or partial writing, record, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, map, or copy thereof. (b) “Representing” means completely or partially describing, depicting, embodying, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (c) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. “Trade secret” includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it when the owner thereof takes measures to prevent it from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access thereto for limited purposes. (d) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction in whole or in part of an article and any note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article or part or portion thereof. (2) Any person who, with intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret, or with an intent to app
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text states 'shall take reasonable measures,' while the modified text uses 'shall endeavor to protect' and 'attempt to take measures,' reducing the enforceability of the obligation. Additionally, the changed text introduces 'confirmed misuse,' creating ambiguity as to who confirms the misuse. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, Florida Statute 812.081 addresses the theft of trade secrets and requires owners to take measures to protect them. The changes in the contract undermine this requirement by creating ambiguity, making it harder to prove that the trade secret was not adequately protected.

perturbation.original_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, shall be and re main the property of the disclosing party and shall be promptly returned to the disclosing party upon the disclosing party's written request.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information which have been disclosed by either party to the other party, and all copies thereof which are in the possession of the other party, should preferably be returned to the disclosing party upon request.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall be promptly returned' to 'should preferably be returned,' the mandatory obligation to return confidential materials is weakened, allowing room for interpretation and potential non-compliance. It is unclear when a return is not preferred.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 542.335 - Protection of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 542.335 pertains to the protection of trade secrets and allows for civil action for misappropriation. If the receiving party does not promptly return the confidential information upon request, as 'should preferably' implies, it could be argued they are misappropriating the trade secrets, hindering the disclosing party's ability to protect their information. This is because the duty to return shifts from a legal obligation to a 'preference'.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall be promptly returned' to 'should preferably be returned' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential information. The LLM-generated Law Explanation argues that this weakening could potentially be seen as misappropriation of trade secrets, hindering the disclosing party's ability to protect their information. Because the duty shifts from a legal obligation to a mere preference, it opens room for argument. This is an example of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction. The absence of snippets doesn't change the assessment, as the core principle is the weakening of a legal duty by introducing ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall not use the Confidential Information of the other party for any purpose except to evaluate and engage in discussions concerning a potential business relationship between the parties. Neither party shall disclose any Confidential Information of the other party to third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will try not to use the Confidential Information of the other party for any purpose except to evaluate and engage in discussions concerning a potential business relationship between the parties. Neither party should disclose any Confidential Information of the other party to third parties, unless absolutely necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not use' to 'will try not to use' weakens the prohibition on using confidential information. Adding 'unless absolutely necessary' to the restriction on disclosing to third parties introduces ambiguity and a large loophole. Who decides what is 'absolutely necessary'?
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Florida Statutes Chapter 688
perturbation.law_explanation: The Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act (FUTSA) provides legal remedies for the misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the prohibition on using and disclosing confidential information, the modified contract increases the risk of misappropriation and makes it more difficult to pursue legal action under FUTSA, as the 'absolutely necessary' exception creates a gray area.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original obligations, making them difficult to enforce. Specifically, changing 'shall not use' to 'will try not to use' and adding 'unless absolutely necessary' creates loopholes. While the snippets do not provide explicit laws violated, the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition states that a legal contradiction happens when 'an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law'. The changed text does exactly that, making it difficult to enforce the confidentiality clauses.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each receiving party hereunder shall surviv e for a period of five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of the receiving party, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each receiving party hereunder should preferably survive for approximately five years after the disclosure of the Confidential Information or until such time as all Confidential Information of the other party disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known, whichever is earlier.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall survive' to 'should preferably survive' makes the duration of the confidentiality obligations non-binding. Replacing 'five years' with 'approximately five years' introduces further ambiguity, allowing a party to argue that a shorter period is acceptable. Removing the clause 'through no action or inaction of the receiving party' means the receiving party will no longer be responsible should confidential information become publicly known through their actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 726.106 - Remedies for creditor's claim.
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly unrelated, this statute is relevant because weakening the confidentiality obligation can impact a company's valuation, potentially affecting its ability to satisfy creditor claims. If a company's trade secrets are not adequately protected due to the weakened language, and the company subsequently faces financial difficulties, creditors may find it more difficult to recover their claims, especially if the company's competitive advantage has been eroded by the disclosure of trade secrets. Section 726.106 outlines remedies for creditors, and this statute becomes more difficult to enforce if trade secrets are made public and a company loses value because of it.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XLI > Chapter 726 > Section 106 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XLI STATUTE OF FRAUDS, FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS, AND GENERAL ASSIGNMENTS Chapter 726 FRAUDULENT TRANSFERS Entire Chapter SECTION 106 Transfers fraudulent as to present creditors. F.S. 726.106 726.106 Transfers fraudulent as to present creditors. — (1) A transfer made or obligation incurred by a debtor is fraudulent as to a creditor whose claim arose before the transfer was made or the obligation was incurred if the debtor made the transfer or incurred the obligation without receiving a reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the transfer or obligation and the debtor was insolvent at that time or the debtor became insolvent as a result of the transfer or obligation. (2) A transfer made by a debtor is fraudulent as to a creditor whose claim arose before the transfer was made if the transfer was made to an insider for an antecedent debt, the debtor was insolvent at that time, and the insider had reasonable cause to believe that the debtor was insolvent. History. — s. 6, ch. 87-79.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language uses 'shall survive', creating a clear obligation, whereas the changed language introduces ambiguity by stating that the obligations 'should preferably survive'. This weakening of the language from mandatory to preferential undermines the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations. The phrase 'approximately five years' further exacerbates this ambiguity. While the provided law snippet 726.106 doesn't directly address confidentiality clauses, the Law Explanation posits that weakened confidentiality obligations can impact a company's valuation, potentially affecting its ability to satisfy creditor claims. Therefore, the change creates a legal contradiction by weakening a binding obligation, potentially impacting the company's financial stability and therefore its ability to meet creditor claims.
